section.title: Israel Oppressed in Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The book opens with a short roll call that hooks Exodus to Genesis, then a rapid time jump: a new king arises, and the seam of the whole book turns on his fear. Two scenes carry the chapter: Pharaoh's policy of crushing labor, and his failed plot through the midwives.
section.thesis: The God who filled the earth through Abraham's family is still multiplying His people even as a frightened empire tries to grind them down, and the fear of God in two lowly midwives outlasts the fear of a king.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the seed of Jacob multiplies in Egypt; a new king enslaves them with forced labor; the king's plot to kill the sons fails before the courage of Shiphrah and Puah.

units.label: A people multiplying
units.note: the promise to Abraham is being kept

verses.text: These are the names of the sons of Israel who went to Egypt with Jacob, each with his family:
Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah;
Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin;
Dan and Naphtali; Gad and Asher.
The descendants of Jacob numbered seventy in all, including Joseph, who was already in Egypt.
Now Joseph and all his brothers and all that generation died,
but the Israelites were {{parah|fruitful}} and increased rapidly; they multiplied and became exceedingly numerous, so that the land was filled with them.

units.label: A new king, a crushing labor

verses.text: Then a new king, who did not know Joseph, came to power in Egypt.
“Look,” he said to his people, “the Israelites have become too numerous and too powerful for us.
Come, let us deal shrewdly with them, or they will increase even more; and if a war breaks out, they may join our enemies, fight against us, and leave the country.”
So the Egyptians appointed taskmasters over the Israelites to oppress them with forced labor. As a result, they built Pithom and Rameses as store cities for Pharaoh.
But the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied and flourished; so the Egyptians came to dread the Israelites.
They worked the Israelites ruthlessly
and made their lives bitter with hard labor in brick and mortar, and with all kinds of work in the fields. Every service they imposed was harsh.

units.label: The midwives fear God
units.note: a plot against the sons fails

verses.text: Then the king of Egypt said to the Hebrew midwives, whose names were Shiphrah and Puah,
“When you help the Hebrew women give birth, observe them on the birthstools. If the child is a son, kill him; but if it is a daughter, let her live.”
The midwives, however, {{yare|feared God}} and did not do as the king of Egypt had instructed; they let the boys live.
So the king of Egypt summoned the midwives and asked them, “Why have you done this? Why have you let the boys live?”
The midwives answered Pharaoh, “The Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women, for they are vigorous and give birth before a midwife arrives.”
So God was good to the midwives, and the people multiplied and became even more numerous.
And because the midwives feared God, He gave them families of their own.
Then Pharaoh commanded all his people, “You must throw every son born to the Hebrews into the Nile, but let every daughter live.”

parah.gloss: To be fruitful. The same word used of the creation blessing in Genesis 1:28 and the promise to Abraham in Genesis 17:6. Under Pharaoh's boot, God's creation and covenant blessing is still quietly at work.

yare.gloss: To fear or revere. The midwives fear God rather than the king, the same word used for the fear of the LORD that is the beginning of wisdom, Proverbs 9:10. Their fear of God overrules their fear of Pharaoh.

basic.context: Exodus opens where Genesis left off: Jacob's family went down to Egypt seventy strong. Centuries pass, they multiply into a nation, and a new Pharaoh who does not remember Joseph turns them into slaves. Even so, God keeps multiplying them, and two brave midwives refuse to kill the baby boys.
basic.hermeneutics: This is straightforward historical narrative describing real events. It is not telling us to disobey every government, but it does show that when a ruler commands what God forbids, faithful people must obey God rather than man, as Shiphrah and Puah did.
basic.originalLanguages: The Israelites are fruitful, parah, the very word for the blessing of creation and of Abraham's covenant. The midwives feared God, yare, the same fear that is the beginning of wisdom. Their fear of God is stronger than their fear of Pharaoh's throne.
basic.typology: Pharaoh's order to drown the sons anticipates Herod's slaughter of the infants in Matthew 2, another tyrant trying to kill the deliverer God is raising up. Both fail, and both point to a hostility toward God's rescuing work that the New Testament traces back to the serpent.
basic.christ: The chapter sets the stage for Israel's exodus, the great Old Testament picture of redemption that Christ Himself fulfills, delivering His people not from Egypt but from sin and death. The attempt to kill Israel's sons foreshadows Herod's attempt on the infant Jesus, the true and greater deliverer.
basic.god: God is quietly sovereign here; His name is not even mentioned until verse 17, yet He is behind the multiplying of His people and behind the courage of two ordinary women. He rewards those who fear Him above earthly power.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 46 lists this same family going to Egypt. Acts 7 retells this history in Stephen's speech. Matthew 2 echoes Pharaoh's plot in Herod's slaughter of the innocents.
basic.oneStory: This is the hinge between the patriarchs and the exodus. A small family becomes an enslaved nation, and God is already at work to deliver them, using the courage of two women who fear Him more than Pharaoh. The rescue that follows becomes the pattern for every rescue God works after it, culminating in Christ.

context.literary: The opening chapter of Exodus, bridging Genesis by repeating the family list of Genesis 46, then compressing centuries into a few verses before slowing down to the midwives' scene, the book's first act of resistance to tyranny.
context.historical: Traditionally dated either to the era of the Hyksos expulsion or several centuries later under Ramesside kings; either way, the text describes a change of dynasty in Egypt that erases Joseph's memory and treats a resident people as a demographic and military threat.
context.canonical: Sets up the entire exodus narrative: the promise to Abraham of numerous descendants, Genesis 15:5, is being fulfilled even under oppression, and the stage is set for the deliverer, Moses, whose birth follows immediately in chapter 2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God's covenant promise of descendants is advancing despite hostile power, and to introduce the conflict between Pharaoh and the LORD that drives the whole book.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes what happened; it is not a manual for every situation. The enduring principle it teaches, echoed in Acts 5:29, is that when human authority commands what God forbids, God's people must obey God.

debates.question: Were the midwives right to deceive Pharaoh?

views.label: Their fear of God, not the lie, is commended
views.case: Verse 17 and 21 credit their fearing God and letting the boys live; the text does not explicitly commend the deception itself.

views.label: The whole action, including the ruse, is approved
views.case: God rewards them with families immediately after their answer to Pharaoh, suggesting the entire scene of resistance is viewed favorably.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage clearly commends refusing to participate in murder even at personal risk, and it leaves room for care in how we handle deception toward those doing evil.

study.originalLanguages: The Israelites are described as parah, fruitful, echoing the creation mandate and the promise to Abraham; no edict of Pharaoh can undo what God has spoken. The midwives yare, feared, God; that fear, not political calculation, explains their courage before the throne.

typology.type: Pharaoh's slaughter of the Hebrew sons
typology.fulfillment: Herod's slaughter of the infants of Bethlehem, Matthew 2:16-18, another tyrant trying and failing to destroy God's appointed deliverer.
The church multiplying under persecution, Acts 8:1-4, a pattern of God's kingdom growing precisely where it is opposed.

study.christ: This chapter has no direct messianic prophecy, but it opens the exodus story that becomes the Old Testament's central picture of redemption, later fulfilled in Christ's greater exodus, Luke 9:31, who delivers His people from sin and death. The attempted murder of Israel's infant sons prefigures Herod's attempt on the infant Christ, both efforts to snuff out God's promised rescuer.
study.god: God is sovereign over empires; a Pharaoh's policy cannot stop the fulfillment of His covenant word. He is also near to the lowly, rewarding two midwives who feared Him rather than the throne. His providence works quietly, unnamed until the courage of ordinary people brings Him into the story.

crossReferences.note: The same family list that opens Exodus, tying the two books together.
Stephen retells this oppression in his defense before the Sanhedrin.
Herod's slaughter of the infants echoes Pharaoh's plot against the Hebrew sons.
We must obey God rather than man, the principle the midwives lived out.

study.oneStory: This is the seedbed of the exodus. A family becomes a nation under the weight of slavery, and God is already moving to deliver them, using the fear of God in two humble midwives to save the very sons through whom the deliverer will come. It is the first move in the great story of redemption that runs to Christ.

quiz.q: Why did the new king of Egypt turn against the Israelites?

options.text: The Israelites asked to be enslaved.
options.note: Nothing in the text suggests this; the oppression is imposed against their will.

options.text: God commanded Pharaoh to oppress them as judgment.
options.note: This reverses the text; God is not the source of the oppression, Pharaoh's fear is.

options.text: They had committed a crime against Egypt.
options.note: The text gives no such accusation; it is fear of numbers and power, not any wrongdoing.

options.text: He feared their growing numbers and strength and wanted to control them.
options.note: Verses 9 to 10 give his own stated fear as the reason for the oppression.

quiz.q: What happened despite Pharaoh's forced labor?

options.text: They stopped having children in protest.
options.note: Not in the text; it records growth despite hardship.

options.text: Their numbers slowly declined.
options.note: The opposite is true; oppression did not stop their growth.

options.text: They were completely wiped out.
options.note: The chapter shows survival and multiplication, not destruction.

options.text: The Israelites multiplied and flourished all the more.
options.note: Verse 12 says the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied.

quiz.q: Why did Shiphrah and Puah refuse to kill the newborn sons?

options.text: They were afraid of being caught by other Hebrews.
options.note: The stated reason is fear of God, not fear of their own people.

options.text: They had no opinion either way.
options.note: Their fear of God is the explicit, deliberate motive given in the text.

options.text: They feared God more than they feared Pharaoh.
options.note: Verse 17 states plainly that the midwives feared God.

options.text: They did not understand Pharaoh's command.
options.note: They understood it clearly and chose not to obey it.

quiz.q: How did God respond to the midwives' courage?

options.text: He punished them for deceiving Pharaoh.
options.note: The text records reward, not punishment, for their fear of God.

options.text: He was good to them and gave them families of their own.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 record God's kindness to them because they feared Him.

options.text: He ignored them entirely.
options.note: God's attention to them is explicit in the text.

options.text: He made them queens of Egypt.
options.note: This is not in the text; their reward was families, not political power.

quiz.q: What does this chapter show about God's covenant promise to Abraham?

options.text: It has nothing to do with this chapter.
options.note: The fruitfulness language directly recalls the Abrahamic promise.

options.text: It had failed because of the slavery.
options.note: The opposite is shown; the promise advances precisely through the hardship.

options.text: It was still being fulfilled even under brutal oppression.
options.note: The Israelites' fruitfulness echoes the promise of Genesis 15 and 17, advancing despite Pharaoh's plans.

options.text: It was replaced by a new promise to Pharaoh.
options.note: Nothing in the text suggests any promise to Pharaoh; the covenant is with Abraham's descendants.

questions: The Israelites flourished even under crushing oppression. Where do you need to trust that God's purposes are still advancing even when your circumstances feel like Pharaoh's brickyards?
Shiphrah and Puah risked their lives because they feared God more than the king. Where is fearing people, rather than fearing God, shaping your choices right now?
Pharaoh's cruelty could not stop the fulfillment of God's promise. What situation in your life feels immovable, and how does this chapter reframe what God can do in it?
God rewarded two ordinary midwives whose names history almost forgot. How does it encourage you that God notices small, costly acts of faithfulness that no one else may ever applaud?
The chapter closes with Pharaoh's most brutal order yet, and still God is at work. Where do you need hope that the darkest verse is never God's last word?

translationNotes.note: The Masoretic Text has seventy descendants, matching Genesis 46:27, while the Dead Sea Scrolls and Greek Septuagint, followed in Acts 7:14, have seventy five, likely counting additional grandsons of Joseph.
The phrase rendered leave the country can also be translated take the country, changing whether Pharaoh fears the Israelites fleeing or seizing power.
Some ancient versions add to the Hebrews to Pharaoh's final command, specifying the sons in view, though the Hebrew text itself does not include that phrase.
